notes: Kastner operated Cornell's IBM System 360/65 and wrote some of the university's first commercial software on it.
Being developed at MIT in the same building where Kastner trained as a computer operator in 1966.
Kastner taught himself to program on IBM mainframes at MIT overnight at $360/hour on his operator account.
IBM OEM of Stratus fault-tolerant hardware; Kastner helped build the marketing for this product.
Kastner spent six years marketing fault-tolerant computing at Stratus, competing against Tandem and IBM.
Kastner led the DECtp initiative at DEC 1987-1988, including a secret midnight benchmark against IBM mainframes.
Kastner was a founding member of TPC, which brought audited transparency to database performance claims.
Aberdeen's TCO modeling frameworks defined gold-standard enterprise technology research through the 1990s and early 2000s.
Kastner consulted on a utility running remote meter reading over power lines at five baud.
Technology used by a utility client of Arthur D. Little Systems; data transmitted at five baud over power lines.
